summary: Jeff Atwood argues that usability testing is essential for software projects but often neglected because teams don't know how to implement it. He recommends Steve Krug's books as accessible entry points for developers to learn usability fundamentals and DIY testing methods. The post also highlights eyetracking research as a valuable reference for teams who can't conduct their own studies. Atwood frames usability as an ongoing battle that requires commitment, but one that can be fought with proper training through these resources.
key_points: Projects without people who care about usability are doomed to fail
Steve Krug's books provide the most accessible introduction to usability for developers
Organizations should learn to do usability testing themselves since most can't afford dedicated professionals
Eyetracking research books can serve as proxy data for teams who can't conduct their own studies
Usability is an endless war, but accessible resources provide basic training for fighting it
money_quotes: Writing code? That's the easy part. Getting your application in the hands of users, and creating applications that people actually want to use – now that's the hard stuff.
If you don't have people that care about usability on your project, your project is doomed.
It's easy to parrot 'Usability is Important!' but you have to walk the walk, too.
Usability testing is one of the best things people can do to improve Web sites (or almost anything they're creating that people have to interact with).
Usability isn't really cheap or easy. It's an endless war, with innumerable battlegrounds, stretching all the way back to the dawn of computing.
These books, at least, are cheap and easy in the sense that they give you some basic training in fighting the good (usability) fight.
key_insight: Usability expertise isn't optional for software teams—it's essential, and accessible books can democratize this knowledge for developers who otherwise wouldn't prioritize it.
